solution_html:
{tex}{P}_{{t}}={P}_{{i}}-2 {x}+2 {x}+{x}={P}_{{i}}+{x}{/tex}
{tex}{x}={P}_{{t}}-{P}_{{i}}{/tex}
{tex}{p}_{{A}}={P}_{{i}}-2 {x}{/tex}

{tex}=3 {P}_{{i}}-2 {P}_{{t}}{/tex}

question_text: The decomposition of NH 3 on platinum surface is zero order reaction. What are the rates of production of N 2 and H 2 if k = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 mol L -1 s -1 ?
explanation: {tex}2N{H_3} \to {N_2} + 3{H_2}{/tex} Rate {tex} = -\frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ {N{H_3}} \right]}}{{dt}} = +\frac{{ d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = + \frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} For zero order reaction rate = k {tex}\therefore - \frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ {N{H_3}} \right]}}{{dt}} = \frac{{d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} Rate of production of {tex}{N_2} = \frac{{d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} Rate of production of {tex}{H_2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = k{/tex} {tex}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = 3k{/tex} {tex} = 3 \times 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex} = 7.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}2N{H_3} \to {N_2} + 3{H_2}{/tex} Rate {tex} = -\frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ {N{H_3}} \right]}}{{dt}} = +\frac{{ d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = + \frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} For zero order reaction rate = k {tex}\therefore - \frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ {N{H_3}} \right]}}{{dt}} = \frac{{d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} Rate of production of {tex}{N_2} = \frac{{d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} Rate of production of {tex}{H_2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = k{/tex} {tex}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = 3k{/tex} {tex} = 3 \times 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex} = 7.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}2N{H_3} \to {N_2} + 3{H_2}{/tex}
Rate {tex} = -\frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ {N{H_3}} \right]}}{{dt}} = +\frac{{ d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex} = + \frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex}
For zero order reaction rate = k
{tex}\therefore - \frac{1}{2}\frac{{d\left[ {N{H_3}} \right]}}{{dt}} = \frac{{d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Rate of production of {tex}{N_2} = \frac{{d\left[ {{N_2}} \right]}}{{dt}}{/tex}
{tex} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Rate of production of {tex}{H_2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{3}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = k{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{d\left[ {{H_2}} \right]}}{{dt}} = 3k{/tex}
{tex} = 3 \times 2.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex} = 7.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
A reaction is second order with respect to a reactant. How is the rate of reaction affected if the concentration of the reactant is:

  1. doubled
  2. reduced to half
question_text: A reaction is second order with respect to a reactant. How is the rate of reaction affected if the concentration of the reactant is: doubled reduced to half
explanation: Let the concentration of the reactant be [A] = a Rate of reaction, {tex} R = k{\left[ A \right]^2}{/tex} {tex} = k{a^2}{/tex} If the concentration of the reactant is doubled, i.e. [A] = 2a, then the rate of the reaction would be {tex}R' = k{(2a)^2} = 4k{a^2}{/tex} = 4R Therefore, the rate of the reaction would increase by 4 times. If the concentration of the reactant is reduced to half, i.e., {tex}[A] = \frac{1}{2}a{/tex} then the rate of the reaction would be {tex} R' = k{\left( {\frac{1}{2}a} \right)^2}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{4}k{a^2} = \frac{1}{4}R {/tex} Therefore, the rate of the reaction would be reduced to {tex}\frac{1}{4}th{/tex} .
solution: Let the concentration of the reactant be [A] = a Rate of reaction, {tex} R = k{\left[ A \right]^2}{/tex} {tex} = k{a^2}{/tex} If the concentration of the reactant is doubled, i.e. [A] = 2a, then the rate of the reaction would be {tex}R' = k{(2a)^2} = 4k{a^2}{/tex} = 4R Therefore, the rate of the reaction would increase by 4 times. If the concentration of the reactant is reduced to half, i.e., {tex}[A] = \frac{1}{2}a{/tex} then the rate of the reaction would be {tex} R' = k{\left( {\frac{1}{2}a} \right)^2}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{4}k{a^2} = \frac{1}{4}R {/tex} Therefore, the rate of the reaction would be reduced to {tex}\frac{1}{4}th{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let the concentration of the reactant be [A] = a
Rate of reaction,{tex} R = k{\left[ A \right]^2}{/tex}{tex} = k{a^2}{/tex}

  1. If the concentration of the reactant is doubled, i.e. [A] = 2a, then the rate of the reaction would be
    {tex}R' = k{(2a)^2} = 4k{a^2}{/tex}
    = 4R
    Therefore, the rate of the reaction would increase by 4 times.
  2. If the concentration of the reactant is reduced to half, i.e.,{tex}[A] = \frac{1}{2}a{/tex} then the rate of the reaction would be {tex} R' = k{\left( {\frac{1}{2}a} \right)^2}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{1}{4}k{a^2} = \frac{1}{4}R {/tex} 
    Therefore, the rate of the reaction would be reduced to {tex}\frac{1}{4}th{/tex}.

question_html: The half life for radioactive decay of 14C is 5730 years. An archaeological artifact containing wood had only 80% of the 14C found in a living tree. Estimate the age of the sample.
question_text: The half life for radioactive decay of 14 C is 5730 years. An archaeological artifact containing wood had only 80% of the 14 C found in a living tree. Estimate the age of the sample.
explanation: Radioactive decay follows first order kinetics, Decay constant (K) {tex} = \frac{{0.693}}{{{t_{1/2}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.693}}{{5730}}yea{r^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \,\frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{0.693/5730\,yea{r^{ - 1}}}}\log \,\frac{{100}}{{80}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303 \times 5730}}{{0.693}} \times 0.0969\,year{/tex} = 1845 years
solution: Radioactive decay follows first order kinetics, Decay constant (K) {tex} = \frac{{0.693}}{{{t_{1/2}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.693}}{{5730}}yea{r^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \,\frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{0.693/5730\,yea{r^{ - 1}}}}\log \,\frac{{100}}{{80}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303 \times 5730}}{{0.693}} \times 0.0969\,year{/tex} = 1845 years
solution_html:
Radioactive decay follows first order kinetics,
Decay constant (K)  {tex} = \frac{{0.693}}{{{t_{1/2}}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.693}}{{5730}}yea{r^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \,\frac{{{{[R]}_0}}}{{[R]}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{{0.693/5730\,yea{r^{ - 1}}}}\log \,\frac{{100}}{{80}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{2.303 \times 5730}}{{0.693}} \times 0.0969\,year{/tex}
= 1845 years

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
A reaction is first order in A and second order in B.

  1. Write the differential rate equation.
  2. How is the rate affected on increasing the concentration of B three times?
  3. How is the rate affected when the concentrations of both A and B are doubled?
question_text: A reaction is first order in A and second order in B. Write the differential rate equation. How is the rate affected on increasing the concentration of B three times? How is the rate affected when the concentrations of both A and B are doubled?
explanation: It is given that a reaction is first order in A and second order in B. The differential rate equation will be {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} If the concentration of B is increased three times, then {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[3B]^2} = 9.k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} Therefore, the rate of reaction will increase 9 times. When the concentrations of both A and B are doubled, {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2} = k[2A]{[2B]^2} = 8.k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} Therefore, the rate of reaction will increase 8 times.
solution: It is given that a reaction is first order in A and second order in B. The differential rate equation will be {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} If the concentration of B is increased three times, then {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[3B]^2} = 9.k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} Therefore, the rate of reaction will increase 9 times. When the concentrations of both A and B are doubled, {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2} = k[2A]{[2B]^2} = 8.k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex} Therefore, the rate of reaction will increase 8 times.
solution_html:
It is given that a reaction is first order in A and second order in B.

  1. The differential rate equation will be
    {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex}
  2. If the concentration of B is increased three times, then
    {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[3B]^2} = 9.k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex}
    Therefore, the rate of reaction will increase 9 times.
  3. When the concentrations of both A and B are doubled,
    {tex} - \frac{{d[R]}}{{dt}} = k[A]{[B]^2} = k[2A]{[2B]^2} = 8.k[A]{[B]^2}{/tex}
    Therefore, the rate of reaction will increase 8 times.

question_text: For the reaction {tex}{A}+{B} \rightarrow{/tex} Products, the following initial rates were obtained at various initial concentrations of reactants: Sl. No. {tex}[A] / {mol} {L}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}[B] / {mol} {L}^{-1}{/tex} Initial rate {tex}/ {mol} {L}^{-1} {~s}^{-1}{/tex} 1 0.1 0.1 0.05 2 0.2 0.1 0.10 3 0.1 0.2 0.05 Determine the order of the reaction with respect to A and B and overall order of the reaction.
explanation: {tex} \text { Rate }={k}[{~A}]^{{x}}[{~B}]^{{y}} {/tex} {tex} \text { Eq. } 1 \text { Rate }_1={k}(0.1)^{{x}}(0.1)^{{y}}=5.0 \times 10^{-2} {/tex} {tex} \text { Eq. } 2 \text { Rate }_2={k}(0.2)^{{x}}(0.1)^{{y}}=1.0 \times 10^{-1} {/tex} {tex} \text { Eq. } 3 \text { Rate }_3={k}(0.1)^{{x}}(0.2)^{{y}}=5.0 \times 10^{-2} {/tex} {tex} \frac{0.1}{0.5}=\frac{k \times 0.2^x \times 0.1^y}{k \times 0.1^x \times 0.1^y} {/tex} {tex} \text { Hence } {x}=1 {/tex} {tex} \frac{0.05}{0.05}=\frac{k \times 0.1^x \times 0.2^y}{k \times 0.1^x \times 0.1^y} {/tex} {tex} \text { Hence } {y}=0 {/tex} {tex} \text { Rate }={k}[{~A}]^1[{~B}]^0 {/tex} {tex} \text { Overall order }=1 {/tex}
solution: {tex} \text { Rate }={k}[{~A}]^{{x}}[{~B}]^{{y}} {/tex} {tex} \text { Eq. } 1 \text { Rate }_1={k}(0.1)^{{x}}(0.1)^{{y}}=5.0 \times 10^{-2} {/tex} {tex} \text { Eq. } 2 \text { Rate }_2={k}(0.2)^{{x}}(0.1)^{{y}}=1.0 \times 10^{-1} {/tex} {tex} \text { Eq. } 3 \text { Rate }_3={k}(0.1)^{{x}}(0.2)^{{y}}=5.0 \times 10^{-2} {/tex} {tex} \frac{0.1}{0.5}=\frac{k \times 0.2^x \times 0.1^y}{k \times 0.1^x \times 0.1^y} {/tex} {tex} \text { Hence } {x}=1 {/tex} {tex} \frac{0.05}{0.05}=\frac{k \times 0.1^x \times 0.2^y}{k \times 0.1^x \times 0.1^y} {/tex} {tex} \text { Hence } {y}=0 {/tex} {tex} \text { Rate }={k}[{~A}]^1[{~B}]^0 {/tex} {tex} \text { Overall order }=1 {/tex}

{tex} \text { Rate }={k}[{~A}]^{{x}}[{~B}]^{{y}} {/tex}
{tex} \text { Eq. } 1 \text { Rate }_1={k}(0.1)^{{x}}(0.1)^{{y}}=5.0 \times 10^{-2} {/tex}
{tex} \text { Eq. } 2 \text { Rate }_2={k}(0.2)^{{x}}(0.1)^{{y}}=1.0 \times 10^{-1} {/tex}
{tex} \text { Eq. } 3 \text { Rate }_3={k}(0.1)^{{x}}(0.2)^{{y}}=5.0 \times 10^{-2} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{0.1}{0.5}=\frac{k \times 0.2^x \times 0.1^y}{k \times 0.1^x \times 0.1^y} {/tex}

question_text: A certain reaction is {tex}50 \%{/tex} complete in 20 minutes at 300 K and the same reaction is {tex}50 \%{/tex} complete in 5 minutes at 350 K. {tex} {k}_2=\frac{0.693}{5}=0.1386 / 1.386 \times 10^{-1} {~min}^{-1} {/tex}
{tex} \log \frac{k_2}{k_1}=\frac{E_{{a}}}{2.303 {R}}\left[\frac{T_2-T_1}{T_1 T_2}\right] {/tex}

question_html:
The decomposition of phosphine, PH3 proceeds according to the following equation:
4PH3(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} P4(g) + 6H2(g)

Rate = k [PH3]
The half-life of PH3 is 37.9 s at 120oC.

  2. What fraction of the original sample of PH3 remains behind after 1 min?
question_text: The decomposition of phosphine, PH 3 proceeds according to the following equation: 4PH 3 (g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} P 4 (g) + 6H 2 (g) It is found that the reaction follows the following rate equation Rate = k [PH 3 ] The half-life of PH 3 is 37.9 s at 120 o C. How much time is required for 3/4th of PH 3 to decompose? What fraction of the original sample of PH 3 remains behind after 1 min?
explanation: Given, t 1/2 = 37.9s, initial concentration= [R] 0 concentration after time, {tex}t=\frac{[R]_{0}}{4}{/tex} (because {tex}\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{th}{/tex} of PH 3 is decomposed). {tex}k=\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{37.9} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{\frac{[R]_{0}}{4}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{\frac{0.693}{37.9}} \log 4{/tex} {tex}\text { or } t=\frac{2.303 \times 37.9 \times 0.6020}{0.693}=75.82 \mathrm{s}{/tex} t = 1 min = 60s and {tex}k=\frac{0.693}{37.9}=0.0183 \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} {tex}60=\frac{2.303}{0.0183} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} or {tex}\log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\frac{60 \times 0.0183}{2.303}=0.4763{/tex} {tex}\frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\text { antilog } 0.4763{/tex} {tex}\frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=2.994{/tex} Let the initial amount is 100 {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{100}{2.994}=[R]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [R] = 33.40%
solution: Given, t 1/2 = 37.9s, initial concentration= [R] 0 concentration after time, {tex}t=\frac{[R]_{0}}{4}{/tex} (because {tex}\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{th}{/tex} of PH 3 is decomposed). {tex}k=\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{37.9} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{\frac{[R]_{0}}{4}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{\frac{0.693}{37.9}} \log 4{/tex} {tex}\text { or } t=\frac{2.303 \times 37.9 \times 0.6020}{0.693}=75.82 \mathrm{s}{/tex} t = 1 min = 60s and {tex}k=\frac{0.693}{37.9}=0.0183 \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} {tex}60=\frac{2.303}{0.0183} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex} or {tex}\log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\frac{60 \times 0.0183}{2.303}=0.4763{/tex} {tex}\frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=\text { antilog } 0.4763{/tex} {tex}\frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}=2.994{/tex} Let the initial amount is 100 {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{100}{2.994}=[R]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [R] = 33.40%
solution_html:
Given, t1/2 = 37.9s, initial concentration= [R]0
concentration after time, {tex}t=\frac{[R]_{0}}{4}{/tex}(because {tex}\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{th}{/tex} of PH3 is decomposed).

  1. {tex}k=\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{37.9} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex}
    {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{\frac{[R]_{0}}{4}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2.303}{\frac{0.693}{37.9}} \log 4{/tex}
    {tex}\text { or } t=\frac{2.303 \times 37.9 \times 0.6020}{0.693}=75.82 \mathrm{s}{/tex}
  2. t = 1 min = 60s and {tex}k=\frac{0.693}{37.9}=0.0183 \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex}
    {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex}
    {tex}60=\frac{2.303}{0.0183} \log \frac{[R]_{0}}{[R]}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
What is known as 'activation energy'. How is the activation energy affected by:

  1. The use of a catalyst and
  2. A rise in temperature?
question_text: What is known as 'activation energy'. How is the activation energy affected by: The use of a catalyst and A rise in temperature?
explanation: Activation energy is the extra energy which must be supplied to the reactants so that they can change into products. It is believed that the catalyst provides an alternate pathway or reaction mechanism by reducing the activation energy between reactants and products and hence lowering the potential energy barrier. There is no effect on activation energy with rise in temperature but number of molecules possessing energy equal to or more than activation energy will increase.
solution: Activation energy is the extra energy which must be supplied to the reactants so that they can change into products. It is believed that the catalyst provides an alternate pathway or reaction mechanism by reducing the activation energy between reactants and products and hence lowering the potential energy barrier. There is no effect on activation energy with rise in temperature but number of molecules possessing energy equal to or more than activation energy will increase.
solution_html:
Activation energy is the extra energy which must be supplied to the reactants so that they can change into products.

  1. It is believed that the catalyst provides an alternate pathway or reaction mechanism by reducing the activation energy between reactants and products and hence lowering the potential energy barrier.
  2. There is no effect on activation energy with rise in temperature but number of molecules possessing energy equal to or more than activation energy will increase.

question_html: A first order decomposition reaction takes 40 minutes for 30% decomposition, calculate t1/2 value for it.
question_text: A first order decomposition reaction takes 40 minutes for 30% decomposition, calculate t 1/2 value for it.
solution_html:
Here t = 40 min.
Let a = 100x = 30% of 100 = 30
Using the formula
{tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{K}\log \frac{a}{{a - x}}{/tex}.
{tex}40 = \frac{{2.303}}{K}\log \frac{{100}}{{100 - 30}}{/tex}
{tex}40 = \frac{{2.303}}{K}\log \frac{{100}}{{70}}{/tex}
{tex}40 = \frac{{2.303}}{K}(\log 10 - \log 7){/tex}
{tex}40 = \frac{{2.303}}{K}(1 - 0.8451){/tex}
{tex}40 = \frac{{2.303}}{K} \times 0.1549{/tex}
{tex}K = \frac{{2.303 \times 0.1549}}{{40}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.357}}{{40}} = 0.0089\,{\min ^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{{0.693}}{K}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.693}}{{0.0089}}{/tex}= 77.86 min

subject: Chemistry
question_html: What is meant by order and molecularity of a reaction? Distinguish between order and molecularity.
question_text: What is meant by order and molecularity of a reaction? Distinguish between order and molecularity.
explanation: Order It is sum of powers to which concentration terms are raised in rate law or rate equation. It is determined experimentally. It can be zero or even in fraction The order of complex reaction can be determined and slowest step is rate determining step. Molecularity It is the number of molecules taking part in the reaction. It is determined theoretically It is always in whole number Molecularity of each step is determined separately.
solution: Order It is sum of powers to which concentration terms are raised in rate law or rate equation. It is determined experimentally. It can be zero or even in fraction The order of complex reaction can be determined and slowest step is rate determining step. Molecularity It is the number of molecules taking part in the reaction. It is determined theoretically It is always in whole number Molecularity of each step is determined separately.
solution_html:
Order

  1. It is sum of powers to which concentration terms are raised in rate law or rate equation.
  2. It is determined experimentally.
  3. It can be zero or even in fraction
  4. The order of complex reaction can be determined and slowest step is rate determining step.

Molecularity

  1. It is the number of molecules taking part in the reaction.
  2. It is determined theoretically
  3. It is always in whole number
  4. Molecularity of each step is determined separately.

question_html:
1. Write the rate law for a first order reaction. Justify the statement that half life for a first order reaction is independent of the initial concentration of the reactant.
  2. For a first order reaction, show that the time required for 99% completion of a first order reaction is twice the time required for the completion of 90%.
question_text: Write the rate law for a first order reaction. Justify the statement that half life for a first order reaction is independent of the initial concentration of the reactant. For a first order reaction, show that the time required for 99% completion of a first order reaction is twice the time required for the completion of 90%.
explanation: For a first order reaction {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[ R ]_{ o }}{[ R ]}{/tex} where [R] o = initial concentration, [R] = conc. after time t When half of the reaction is completed, [R] = {tex}\frac {[R]_o}{2}{/tex} . Representing, the time taken for half of the reaction to be completed, by {tex}t_\frac 12{/tex} , equation becomes: {tex}k =\frac{2.303}{t_\frac {1 }{ 2}} \log \frac{[ R ]_{ o }}{\frac {[ R ]_{ o }}{ 2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow t_\frac{1 }{ 2} =\frac{2.303}{k} \log 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow t_\frac{1 }{ 2} =\frac{2.303}{k} \times 0.3010{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow t_\frac{1 }{ 2} =\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex} The above equation shows that half-life of first order reaction is independent of the initial concentration of the reactant. For a first order reaction {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex} {tex}t_{99 \%} =\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{k} \log 100{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303 \times 2}{k}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4.606}{k}{/tex} and {tex}t_{90 \%} =\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{10}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{k} \log 10=\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} {tex}\frac{t_{99 \%}}{t_{90 \%}} =2{/tex} {tex}t_{99 \%} =2 \times t_{90 \%}{/tex}
solution: For a first order reaction {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[ R ]_{ o }}{[ R ]}{/tex} where [R] o = initial concentration, [R] = conc. after time t When half of the reaction is completed, [R] = {tex}\frac {[R]_o}{2}{/tex} . Representing, the time taken for half of the reaction to be completed, by {tex}t_\frac 12{/tex} , equation becomes: {tex}k =\frac{2.303}{t_\frac {1 }{ 2}} \log \frac{[ R ]_{ o }}{\frac {[ R ]_{ o }}{ 2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow t_\frac{1 }{ 2} =\frac{2.303}{k} \log 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow t_\frac{1 }{ 2} =\frac{2.303}{k} \times 0.3010{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow t_\frac{1 }{ 2} =\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex} The above equation shows that half-life of first order reaction is independent of the initial concentration of the reactant. For a first order reaction {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex} {tex}t_{99 \%} =\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{k} \log 100{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303 \times 2}{k}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4.606}{k}{/tex} and {tex}t_{90 \%} =\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{10}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303}{k} \log 10=\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} {tex}\frac{t_{99 \%}}{t_{90 \%}} =2{/tex} {tex}t_{99 \%} =2 \times t_{90 \%}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. For a first order reaction
    {tex}k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[ R ]_{ o }}{[ R ]}{/tex}
    where [R]o = initial concentration, [R] = conc. after time t
    When half of the reaction is completed, [R] = {tex}\frac {[R]_o}{2}{/tex}.
    Representing, the time taken for half of the reaction to be completed, by {tex}t_\frac 12{/tex}, equation becomes:
    {tex}k =\frac{2.303}{t_\frac {1 }{ 2}} \log \frac{[ R ]_{ o }}{\frac {[ R ]_{ o }}{ 2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow t_\frac{1 }{ 2} =\frac{2.303}{k} \log 2{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow t_\frac{1 }{ 2} =\frac{2.303}{k} \times 0.3010{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow t_\frac{1 }{ 2} =\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex}
    The above equation shows that half-life of first order reaction is independent of the initial concentration of the reactant.
  2. For a first order reaction
    {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{a}{a-x}{/tex}
    {tex}t_{99 \%} =\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{1}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2.303}{k} \log 100{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2.303 \times 2}{k}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{4.606}{k}{/tex}
    and {tex}t_{90 \%} =\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{10}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2.303}{k} \log 10=\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{t_{99 \%}}{t_{90 \%}} =2{/tex}
    {tex}t_{99 \%} =2 \times t_{90 \%}{/tex}

question_html: For a first order reaction, show that time required for 99% completion is twice than the time required for the completion of 90% of reaction.
question_text: For a first order reaction, show that time required for 99% completion is twice than the time required for the completion of 90% of reaction.
explanation: For first order reaction, {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - x}}{/tex} where k = rate constant, a = initial amount of reactant, a - x = amount of reactant left after time t 99% completion means that x = 99 % of a = 0.99 a {tex}t_{99\%} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - 0.99a}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log {10^2} = 2 \times \frac{{2.303}}{k}{/tex} Now for 90% completion: x = 90% of a = 0.90 a {tex}t_{90\%} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - 0.90a}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log 10 = \frac{{2.303}}{k}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{t_{99\% }}}}{{{t_{90\% }}}} = \left( {\frac{{2 \times 2.303}}{k}} \right)/\frac{{2.303}}{k}{/tex} t 99% {tex} = 2 \times t_{90\% }{/tex}
solution: For first order reaction, {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - x}}{/tex} where k = rate constant, a = initial amount of reactant, a - x = amount of reactant left after time t 99% completion means that x = 99 % of a = 0.99 a {tex}t_{99\%} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - 0.99a}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log {10^2} = 2 \times \frac{{2.303}}{k}{/tex} Now for 90% completion: x = 90% of a = 0.90 a {tex}t_{90\%} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - 0.90a}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log 10 = \frac{{2.303}}{k}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{t_{99\% }}}}{{{t_{90\% }}}} = \left( {\frac{{2 \times 2.303}}{k}} \right)/\frac{{2.303}}{k}{/tex} t 99% {tex} = 2 \times t_{90\% }{/tex}
solution_html:
For first order reaction, {tex}t = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - x}}{/tex}

where k = rate constant, a = initial amount of reactant, a - x = amount of reactant left after time t
99% completion means that
x = 99 % of a = 0.99 a
{tex}t_{99\%} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - 0.99a}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log {10^2} = 2 \times \frac{{2.303}}{k}{/tex}
Now for  90% completion:
x = 90% of a = 0.90 a
{tex}t_{90\%} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{a}{{a - 0.90a}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log 10 = \frac{{2.303}}{k}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{{t_{99\% }}}}{{{t_{90\% }}}} = \left( {\frac{{2 \times 2.303}}{k}} \right)/\frac{{2.303}}{k}{/tex}
 t99% {tex} = 2 \times t_{90\% }{/tex}

question_text: During nuclear explosion, one of the products is 90 Sr with half-life of 28.1 years. If 1 {tex}\mu g{/tex} of 90 Sr was absorbed in the bones of a newly born baby instead of calcium, how much of it will remain after 10 years and 60 years if it is not lost metabolically.
explanation: Here, {tex} k=\frac{0.693}{t{}^{1}/{}_{2}}=\frac{0.693}{28.1}{{y}^{-1}}{/tex} It is known that, {tex} t=\frac{2.303}{k}\log \frac{{{[R]}_{0}}}{[R]}{/tex} {tex}10 = \frac{{2.303}}{{\frac{{0.693}}{{28.1}}}}\log \frac{1}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex}10 = \frac{{2.303}}{{\frac{{0.693}}{{28.1}}}}\left( { - \log [R]} \right){/tex} {tex}\log [R] = - \frac{{10 \times 0.693}}{{2.303 \times 28.1}}{/tex} [R] = antilog (- 0.1071) {tex}= anti\log \left( {\bar 1.8929} \right){/tex} {tex}=0.7814\mu g{/tex} Therefore, {tex} =0.7814\mu g{/tex} of {tex}^{90}Sr{/tex} will remain after 10 years. Again, {tex}t=\frac{2.303}{k}\log \frac{{{[R]}_{0}}}{[R]}{/tex} {tex}60 = \frac{{2.303}}{{\frac{{0.693}}{{28.1}}}}\log \frac{1}{{[R]}}{/tex} {tex}\log [R] = \frac{{60 \times 0.693}}{{2.303 \times 28.1}}{/tex} [R] = antilog ( 0.6425) {tex} = anti\log \left( {\bar 1.3575} \right){/tex} {tex} = 0.2278\mu g{/tex} Therefore, {tex}0.2278\mu g,of ^{90}Sr{/tex} will remain after 60 years.

question_html:
In a reaction between A and B the initial rate of reaction {tex}\left( {{r_0}} \right){/tex}was measured for different initial concentrations of A and B as given below:

A/mol {tex}{L^{ - 1}}{/tex} 0.20 0.20 0.40
B/mol {tex}{L^{ - 1}}{/tex} 0.30 0.10 0.05
{tex}{r_0}/mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}5.07 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex} {tex}5.07 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex} {tex}1.43 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex}

What is the order of the reaction with respect to A and B?
question_text: In a reaction between A and B the initial rate of reaction {tex}\left( {{r_0}} \right){/tex} was measured for different initial concentrations of A and B as given below: A/mol {tex}{L^{ - 1}}{/tex} 0.20 0.20 0.40 B/mol {tex}{L^{ - 1}}{/tex} 0.30 0.10 0.05 {tex}{r_0}/mol\,\,{L^{ - 1}}{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}5.07 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex} {tex}5.07 \times {10^{ - 5}}{/tex} {tex}1.43 \times {10^{ - 4}}{/tex} What is the order of the reaction with respect to A and B?
explanation: Let the rate equation be {tex}Rate= k{\left[ A \right]^x}{\left[ B \right]^y}{/tex} where x and y are orders of reaction with respect to A and B respectively Substituting values from the column 1 and 2 from the table in rate equation {tex}5.07 \times {10^{ - 5}} = k{\left[ {0.2} \right]^x}{\left[ {0.3} \right]^y}...(i){/tex} {tex}5.07 \times {10^{ - 5}} = k{\left[ {0.2} \right]^x}{\left[ {0.1} \right]^y}...(ii){/tex} Dividing (i) by (ii) we get {tex}1 = {3^y} = {3^0} = {3^y} \Rightarrow y = 0{/tex} Again substituting values from column 2 and 3 from the table in rate equation {tex}5.0 \times {10^{ - 5}} = k{\left[ {0.2} \right]^x}{\left[ {0.1} \right]^0}...(iii){/tex} {tex}1.43 \times {10^{ - 4}} = k{\left[ {0.4} \right]^x}{\left[ {0.05} \right]^0}...(iv){/tex} Dividing (iii) by (iv) we get {tex}\frac{1}{{2.8}} = \frac{1}{{{2^x}}}{/tex} 2.8 = 2x log 2.8 = x log 2 {tex}x = \frac{{\log 2.8}}{{\log 2.0}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.4472}}{{0.3010}} = 1.48{/tex} = 1.5 Thus the order of reaction is 1.5 w.r.t A and zero w.r.t B.
solution: Let the rate equation be {tex}Rate= k{\left[ A \right]^x}{\left[ B \right]^y}{/tex} where x and y are orders of reaction with respect to A and B respectively Substituting values from the column 1 and 2 from the table in rate equation {tex}5.07 \times {10^{ - 5}} = k{\left[ {0.2} \right]^x}{\left[ {0.3} \right]^y}...(i){/tex} {tex}5.07 \times {10^{ - 5}} = k{\left[ {0.2} \right]^x}{\left[ {0.1} \right]^y}...(ii){/tex} Dividing (i) by (ii) we get {tex}1 = {3^y} = {3^0} = {3^y} \Rightarrow y = 0{/tex} Again substituting values from column 2 and 3 from the table in rate equation {tex}5.0 \times {10^{ - 5}} = k{\left[ {0.2} \right]^x}{\left[ {0.1} \right]^0}...(iii){/tex} {tex}1.43 \times {10^{ - 4}} = k{\left[ {0.4} \right]^x}{\left[ {0.05} \right]^0}...(iv){/tex} Dividing (iii) by (iv) we get {tex}\frac{1}{{2.8}} = \frac{1}{{{2^x}}}{/tex} 2.8 = 2x log 2.8 = x log 2 {tex}x = \frac{{\log 2.8}}{{\log 2.0}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.4472}}{{0.3010}} = 1.48{/tex} = 1.5 Thus the order of reaction is 1.5 w.r.t A and zero w.r.t B.
solution_html:
Let the rate equation be

{tex}Rate= k{\left[ A \right]^x}{\left[ B \right]^y}{/tex}

where x and y are orders of reaction with respect to A and B respectively

Substituting values from the column 1 and 2 from the table in rate equation
{tex}5.07 \times {10^{ - 5}} = k{\left[ {0.2} \right]^x}{\left[ {0.3} \right]^y}...(i){/tex}
{tex}5.07 \times {10^{ - 5}} = k{\left[ {0.2} \right]^x}{\left[ {0.1} \right]^y}...(ii){/tex}
Dividing (i) by (ii) we get
{tex}1 = {3^y} = {3^0} = {3^y} \Rightarrow y = 0{/tex}

Again substituting values from column 2 and 3 from the table in rate equation
{tex}5.0 \times {10^{ - 5}} = k{\left[ {0.2} \right]^x}{\left[ {0.1} \right]^0}...(iii){/tex}
{tex}1.43 \times {10^{ - 4}} = k{\left[ {0.4} \right]^x}{\left[ {0.05} \right]^0}...(iv){/tex}
Dividing (iii) by (iv) we get
{tex}\frac{1}{{2.8}} = \frac{1}{{{2^x}}}{/tex}
2.8 = 2x
log 2.8 = x log 2
{tex}x = \frac{{\log 2.8}}{{\log 2.0}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.4472}}{{0.3010}} = 1.48{/tex}
= 1.5
Thus the order of reaction is 1.5 w.r.t A and zero w.r.t B.

question_html:
1. The half life for the decomposition of nitramide is 2.1 hours at 15°C.
    {tex}N{H_2}N{O_2}(aq) \to {N_2}O(g) + {H_2}O(l){/tex} . If 6.2 g of {tex}N{H_2}N{O_2}{/tex} decomposes, calculate:
    1. Time taken for 99% decomposition of {tex}N{H_2}N{O_2}{/tex}
    2. Volume of {tex}{N_2}O{/tex} (dry) produced at STP
  2. The conversion of X to Y follows second order kinetics. If concentration of 'X' is increased to three times how will it affect the rate of formation of 'Y' and why?
question_text: The half life for the decomposition of nitramide is 2.1 hours at 15°C. {tex}N{H_2}N{O_2}(aq) \to {N_2}O(g) + {H_2}O(l){/tex} . If 6.2 g of {tex}N{H_2}N{O_2}{/tex} decomposes, calculate: Time taken for 99% decomposition of {tex}N{H_2}N{O_2}{/tex} Volume of {tex}{N_2}O{/tex} (dry) produced at STP The conversion of X to Y follows second order kinetics. If concentration of 'X' is increased to three times how will it affect the rate of formation of 'Y' and why?
explanation: Given half life t 1/2 for decomposition of nitramide=2.1 h {tex}k = \frac{{0.693}}{{{t_{1/2}}}} = \frac{{0.693}}{{2.1h}}{/tex} {tex} = 0.33\;h{^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}{t_{99\% }} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{100}}{{100 - 99}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{K}\log \frac{{100}}{{100 - 99}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}(\log 100-log1)=\frac{{2.303}}{k}\log 100=\frac{{2.303}}{k}\times 2{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{4.606}}{{0.33h{^{ - 1}}}} = 13.96\,h{/tex} Amount decomposed = 99% of 6.2 g {tex} = \frac{{6.2 \times 99}}{{100}}{/tex} = 6.138 g 1 mole of {tex}N{H_2}N{O_2}{/tex} (62 g) produces 22.4 L at STP 6.138 g will produce {tex} = \frac{{22.4}}{{61}} \times 6.138{/tex} = 2.217 L at STP {tex}X \to Y{/tex} Rate {tex} = k{\left[ \text X \right]^2}{/tex} {tex}Rate' = k{\left[ {3\text X} \right]^2}{/tex} when concentration of X is increased to 3 times the original concentration {tex}=9k[X]^2=9\times Rate{/tex} The rate of formation of 'Y' will increase to 9 times the original rate because reaction is second order and concentration is increased 3 times.

solution_html:
1. Given half life t1/2 for decomposition of nitramide=2.1 h
    1. {tex}k = \frac{{0.693}}{{{t_{1/2}}}} = \frac{{0.693}}{{2.1h}}{/tex}
      {tex} = 0.33\;h{^{ - 1}}{/tex}
      {tex}{t_{99\% }} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}\log \frac{{100}}{{100 - 99}}{/tex}
      {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{K}\log \frac{{100}}{{100 - 99}}{/tex}
      {tex} = \frac{{2.303}}{k}(\log 100-log1)=\frac{{2.303}}{k}\log 100=\frac{{2.303}}{k}\times 2{/tex}
      {tex} = \frac{{4.606}}{{0.33h{^{ - 1}}}} = 13.96\,h{/tex}
    2. Amount decomposed = 99% of 6.2 g
      {tex} = \frac{{6.2 \times 99}}{{100}}{/tex} = 6.138 g
      1 mole of {tex}N{H_2}N{O_2}{/tex} (62 g) produces 22.4 L at STP
      6.138 g will produce{tex} = \frac{{22.4}}{{61}} \times 6.138{/tex}
      = 2.217 L at STP
  2. {tex}X \to Y{/tex}
    Rate {tex} = k{\left[ \text X \right]^2}{/tex}
    {tex}Rate' = k{\left[ {3\text X} \right]^2}{/tex} when concentration of X is increased to 3 times the original concentration
    {tex}=9k[X]^2=9\times Rate{/tex}
    The rate of formation of 'Y' will increase to 9 times the original rate because reaction is second order and concentration is increased 3 times.
